petitioner_background: The petitioner is a former professor in the field of education seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). The Texas Service Center director denied the petition and dismissed two subsequent motions. The petitioner appealed to the AAO, which dismissed the appeal and reaffirmed that decision in three prior motion adjudications. This matter concerns a fourth motion to reconsider.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO denied the motion to reconsider because the petitioner failed to support it with pertinent precedent decisions or legal citations demonstrating that the prior decision was based on an incorrect application of law or USCIS policy, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(3). The petitioner's argument that the AAO applied an incorrect standard of proof failed because the petitioner did not explain how the AAO's analysis involved a higher standard than preponderance of the evidence, and the AAO had explained at length in prior decisions why the petitioner had not shown it was more likely than not that he qualified. The petitioner's reliance on a January 28, 2015, non-precedent decision involving a competitive runner was misplaced because non-precedent decisions are only binding on the parties to those cases, the field of education is not sufficiently analogous to competitive athletics, and the petitioner mischaracterized the athlete's record of achievement. The AAO also declined to conduct another de novo review and denied the request for oral argument, noting there is no regulatory provision for oral argument on motion and the petitioner identified no unique factors or legal issues requiring it.
legal_citations: USCIS Policy Memorandum PM-602-0086.1, Precedent and Non-Precedent Decisions of the Administrative Appeals Office (AAO), November 18, 2013
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues only: this is a fourth motion to reconsider following dismissal of an appeal and three prior motions. The AAO noted that a motion to reconsider cannot be used to raise legal arguments that could have been raised earlier, nor to resubmit essentially the same brief and generally allege error. The petitioner's request for oral argument was denied because there is no regulatory provision for oral argument on motion under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a), and the petitioner failed to identify unique factors or legal issues warranting it. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
